title: Wood ash surface decoration
subtitle: Applying wood ash to bisqueware for a natural flux surface effect
excerpt: Wood ash brushed or sprinkled onto bisqueware melts in the kiln and creates a natural glassy surface with a matte, rough, or glossy quality depending on the clay body and firing temperature. This tutorial covers preparing ash, applying it safely, and what to expect from the fired surface at cone 6 and cone 10.
sourceNotes: Wood ash glaze technique has origins in traditional Japanese and Chinese ceramics. Technique derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical ash glaze reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: A glaze or surface finish made using wood ash as a flux, which melts at kiln temperatures to form a natural glassy surface.
A material that lowers the melting point of a glaze mixture, allowing it to fuse at kiln temperatures.
Clay fired once to cone 06, producing a porous surface that holds ash and glaze before the glaze firing.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work. Cone 6 is roughly 1220 degrees C; cone 10 is roughly 1280 degrees C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Wood ash also contains alkali compounds that are caustic. Wear nitrile gloves and a P100 respirator when handling dry ash or ash solutions. Wet-clean all surfaces. Never handle dry ash in a closed room; wet-sieve or work outdoors. Never open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C.
Wood ash contains silica, calcium, potassium, and small amounts of other minerals that act as a natural
flux

at high temperatures. When ash lands on or is applied to a clay surface in the kiln, it melts and bonds with the clay body to produce a surface that ranges from a rough, earthy texture at cone 6 to a flowing, glossy coat at cone 10. The
ash glaze
tradition is central to East Asian ceramics.
When to use it

Wood ash application suits functional stoneware where a natural, irregular surface is desirable. Results are not uniform. Each piece fires differently, depending on the ash type, clay body, temperature, and kiln placement. This unpredictability is the point.
What you need
Hardwood ash (oak, ash tree, or fruit wood): sieve wet through 80 mesh to remove large particles and debris
Bisque-fired stoneware piece
Soft flat brush
Wide flat container for the ash wash
Pottery sponge for cleaning the foot
Step-by-step

Prepare the ash wash: mix sieved ash with water to a thin slurry consistency. Allow to settle for 1-2 hours; the coarsest material sinks and the usable ash wash floats above. Pour off and use the upper layer.
Apply to the
bisque
surface with a broad brush in two coats, allowing each to dry. The ash wash should coat the surface but not drip. For an accent application, brush ash wash only on the upper half of the piece.
For a dripped effect, hold the piece over a container and ladle ash wash over the rim, allowing it to run down the sides and pool at the base.
Allow the piece to dry fully. Clean the foot completely: ash that melts on the kiln shelf will fuse the piece permanently. Use a sponge, not a brush.
Fire to
cone
6 for a matte-to-satin surface. At cone 10 the ash runs more freely and the glaze becomes glossy.
What it looks like when it is working

At cone 6 the ash produces a rough, greenish-tan surface where it melts, and the clay body shows through at thin application areas. The texture is raw and earthy rather than polished. At cone 10 the surface is glossier and the ash runs in rivulets down the sides.
Common mistakes
Ash too thick: pools of ash glass run down the piece and onto the kiln shelf. Apply thinly and check the foot is clean.
Ash from softwood (pine, spruce): softwood ash contains more resin and oils and produces less reliable results. Use hardwood only.
Piece placed too close to another piece in the kiln: molten ash can fuse adjacent pieces together. Leave at least 30 mm clearance between pieces.
